code.description:
A unique identifier for the type of error.

This should be a short, machine-readable string that can be used
by clients to programmatically handle different error types.
Examples: "NOT_FOUND", "VALIDATION_ERROR", "TIMEOUT"

context.description:
Additional diagnostic information about the error context.

This optional field can contain structured data providing more details
about the error, such as validation errors, request parameters that
caused the issue, or other relevant context information.

httpStatusCode.description:
HTTP status code for this error.

Used when converting the error to an HTTP response. If None, falls back to
the error type's default status code or 500.

internal.description:
Indicates if this is an internal error that should not be exposed to users.

When `true`, this error contains sensitive information or implementation
details that should not be shown to end-users. Such errors should be
logged for debugging but replaced with generic error messages in responses.

message.description:
Human-readable error message.

This message should be clear and actionable for developers or end-users,
providing context about what went wrong and potentially how to fix it.

requestId.description: Request ID echoed in the x-request-id response header and server logs.

retryable.description:
Indicates whether the operation that caused the error should be retried.

When `true`, the error is transient and the operation might succeed
if attempted again. When `false`, retrying the same operation is
unlikely to succeed without changes.

source.description:
The underlying error that caused this error, creating an error chain.

This allows for proper error propagation and debugging by maintaining
the full context of how an error occurred through multiple layers
of an application.

responseSizeBytes.description: Size of command response in bytes

state.description: Command states in the Commands protocol lifecycle

target.description: Resource the command is addressed to; null on commands created before target routing

resourceId.description: The resource ID within the deployment's stack (e.g. a Worker/Container/Daemon id).

resourceType.description: The kind of command-capable resource a command targets.

stackSettings.description:
User-customizable deployment settings specified at deploy time.

These settings are provided by the customer via CloudFormation parameters,
Terraform attributes, CLI flags, or Helm values. They customize how the
deployment runs and what capabilities are enabled.

**Key distinction**: StackSettings is user-customizable, while ManagementConfig
is platform-derived (from the Manager's ServiceAccount).

oneOf.description:
Deployment-time compute choices for Alien-managed compute pools.

Application source declares portable pool requirements. This settings
object stores the concrete choices made for one deployment, such as the
provider machine type and selected machine counts.

additionalProperties.description: User-selected deployment settings for one compute pool.

machine.description: Provider machine type selected for this deployment.
Provider machine type selected for this deployment.

pools.description: Selected compute choices keyed by pool ID.

deploymentModel.description: Deployment model: how updates are delivered to the remote environment.

oneOf.description:
Domain configuration for the stack.

When `custom_domains` is set, the specified resources use customer-provided
domains and certificates. Otherwise, Alien auto-generates domains.

additionalProperties.description: Custom domain configuration for a single resource.

certificate.description: Platform-specific certificate references for custom domains.

namespace.description: Secret namespace. Defaults to the release namespace when omitted.

domain.description: Fully qualified domain name to use.

customDomains.description: Custom domain configuration per resource ID.

cnameTarget.description: DNS name or URL for the external load balancer.

externalBindings.description:
External bindings for pre-existing infrastructure.
Allows using existing resources (MinIO, Redis, shared Container Apps
Environment, etc.) instead of having Alien provision them.
Required for Kubernetes platform, optional for cloud platforms.

heartbeats.description: How heartbeat health checks are handled.

oneOf.description:
Kubernetes runtime substrate configuration.

This controls how setup chooses the cluster backing `Platform::Kubernetes`
deployments. When omitted, cloud-backed Kubernetes deployments default to a
managed cluster and generic/on-prem Kubernetes defaults to an external
cluster.
Kubernetes cluster setup settings.
Optional provider-specific identity for a cloud-backed Kubernetes cluster.

oneOf.description:
Canonical error container that provides a structured way to represent errors
with rich metadata including error codes, human-readable messages, context,
and chaining capabilities for error propagation.

This struct is designed to be both machine-readable and user-friendly,
supporting serialization for API responses and detailed error reporting
in distributed systems.

code.description:
A unique identifier for the type of error.

This should be a short, machine-readable string that can be used
by clients to programmatically handle different error types.
Examples: "NOT_FOUND", "VALIDATION_ERROR", "TIMEOUT"

context.description:
Additional diagnostic information about the error context.

This optional field can contain structured data providing more details
about the error, such as validation errors, request parameters that
caused the issue, or other relevant context information.

httpStatusCode.description:
HTTP status code for this error.

Used when converting the error to an HTTP response. If None, falls back to
the error type's default status code or 500.

internal.description:
Indicates if this is an internal error that should not be exposed to users.

When `true`, this error contains sensitive information or implementation
details that should not be shown to end-users. Such errors should be
logged for debugging but replaced with generic error messages in responses.

message.description:
Human-readable error message.

This message should be clear and actionable for developers or end-users,
providing context about what went wrong and potentially how to fix it.

retryable.description:
Indicates whether the operation that caused the error should be retried.

When `true`, the error is transient and the operation might succeed
if attempted again. When `false`, retrying the same operation is
unlikely to succeed without changes.

source.description:
The underlying error that caused this error, creating an error chain.

This allows for proper error propagation and debugging by maintaining
the full context of how an error occurred through multiple layers
of an application.

lastFailedState.description:
Stores the controller state that failed, used for manual retry operations.
This allows resuming from the exact point where the failure occurred.
Stored as JSON to make the struct serializable and movable to alien-core.

error.description:
Canonical error container that provides a structured way to represent errors
with rich metadata including error codes, human-readable messages, context,
and chaining capabilities for error propagation.

This struct is designed to be both machine-readable and user-friendly,
supporting serialization for API responses and detailed error reporting
in distributed systems.

code.description:
A unique identifier for the type of error.

This should be a short, machine-readable string that can be used
by clients to programmatically handle different error types.
Examples: "NOT_FOUND", "VALIDATION_ERROR", "TIMEOUT"

context.description:
Additional diagnostic information about the error context.

This optional field can contain structured data providing more details
about the error, such as validation errors, request parameters that
caused the issue, or other relevant context information.

httpStatusCode.description:
HTTP status code for this error.

Used when converting the error to an HTTP response. If None, falls back to
the error type's default status code or 500.

internal.description:
Indicates if this is an internal error that should not be exposed to users.

When `true`, this error contains sensitive information or implementation
details that should not be shown to end-users. Such errors should be
logged for debugging but replaced with generic error messages in responses.

message.description:
Human-readable error message.

This message should be clear and actionable for developers or end-users,
providing context about what went wrong and potentially how to fix it.

retryable.description:
Indicates whether the operation that caused the error should be retried.

When `true`, the error is transient and the operation might succeed
if attempted again. When `false`, retrying the same operation is
unlikely to succeed without changes.

source.description:
The underlying error that caused this error, creating an error chain.

This allows for proper error propagation and debugging by maintaining
the full context of how an error occurred through multiple layers
of an application.

id.description: Unique identifier for the domain endpoint.

secret.description: Whether the original input is secret.

maxDeployments.description: Maximum number of deployments for newly created groups

project.description: Project ID or name this deployment group belongs to

targetResources.description: Target resource patterns (null = all resources, array = wildcard patterns)

error.description:
Canonical error container that provides a structured way to represent errors
with rich metadata including error codes, human-readable messages, context,
and chaining capabilities for error propagation.

This struct is designed to be both machine-readable and user-friendly,
supporting serialization for API responses and detailed error reporting
in distributed systems.

code.description:
A unique identifier for the type of error.

This should be a short, machine-readable string that can be used
by clients to programmatically handle different error types.
Examples: "NOT_FOUND", "VALIDATION_ERROR", "TIMEOUT"

context.description:
Additional diagnostic information about the error context.

This optional field can contain structured data providing more details
about the error, such as validation errors, request parameters that
caused the issue, or other relevant context information.

httpStatusCode.description:
HTTP status code for this error.

Used when converting the error to an HTTP response. If None, falls back to
the error type's default status code or 500.

internal.description:
Indicates if this is an internal error that should not be exposed to users.

When `true`, this error contains sensitive information or implementation
details that should not be shown to end-users. Such errors should be
logged for debugging but replaced with generic error messages in responses.

message.description:
Human-readable error message.

This message should be clear and actionable for developers or end-users,
providing context about what went wrong and potentially how to fix it.

retryable.description:
Indicates whether the operation that caused the error should be retried.

When `true`, the error is transient and the operation might succeed
if attempted again. When `false`, retrying the same operation is
unlikely to succeed without changes.

source.description:
The underlying error that caused this error, creating an error chain.

This allows for proper error propagation and debugging by maintaining
the full context of how an error occurred through multiple layers
of an application.

phase.description:
Phase of a deployment at which a failure occurred.

Derived from the source deployment status: `preflights-failed` →
`Preflights`, `provisioning-failed` → `Provisioning`, `update-failed` →
`Updating`, `delete-failed` → `Deleting`.
`refresh-failed` is modelled separately via `DeploymentDegraded`.

stackSettings.description:
User-customizable deployment settings specified at deploy time.

These settings are provided by the customer via CloudFormation parameters,
Terraform attributes, CLI flags, or Helm values. They customize how the
deployment runs and what capabilities are enabled.

**Key distinction**: StackSettings is user-customizable, while ManagementConfig
is platform-derived (from the Manager's ServiceAccount).

oneOf.description:
Deployment-time compute choices for Alien-managed compute pools.

Application source declares portable pool requirements. This settings
object stores the concrete choices made for one deployment, such as the
provider machine type and selected machine counts.

additionalProperties.description: User-selected deployment settings for one compute pool.

machine.description: Provider machine type selected for this deployment.
Provider machine type selected for this deployment.

pools.description: Selected compute choices keyed by pool ID.

deploymentModel.description: Deployment model: how updates are delivered to the remote environment.

oneOf.description:
Domain configuration for the stack.

When `custom_domains` is set, the specified resources use customer-provided
domains and certificates. Otherwise, Alien auto-generates domains.

certificate.description: Platform-specific certificate references for custom domains.

externalBindings.description:
External bindings for pre-existing infrastructure.
Allows using existing resources (MinIO, Redis, shared Container Apps
Environment, etc.) instead of having Alien provision them.
Required for Kubernetes platform, optional for cloud platforms.

oneOf.description:
Kubernetes runtime substrate configuration.

This controls how setup chooses the cluster backing `Platform::Kubernetes`
deployments. When omitted, cloud-backed Kubernetes deployments default to a
managed cluster and generic/on-prem Kubernetes defaults to an external
cluster.
Kubernetes cluster setup settings.
Optional provider-specific identity for a cloud-backed Kubernetes cluster.

config.description: Resource that can hold any resource type in the Alien system. All resources share common 'type' and 'id' fields with additional type-specific properties.

id.description: The unique identifier for this specific resource instance. Must contain only alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores ([A-Za-z0-9-_]). Maximum 64 characters.

type.description: Resource type identifier that determines the specific kind of resource. This field is used for polymorphic deserialization and resource-specific behavior.

dependencies.description:
Additional dependencies for this resource beyond those defined in the resource itself.
The total dependencies are: resource.get_dependencies() + this list

items.description:
New ResourceRef that works with any resource type.
This can eventually replace the enum-based ResourceRef for full extensibility.

type.description: Resource type identifier that determines the specific kind of resource. This field is used for polymorphic deserialization and resource-specific behavior.

lifecycle.description: Describes the lifecycle of a resource within a stack, determining how it's managed and deployed.

remoteAccess.description:
Enable remote bindings for this resource (BYOB use case).
When true, binding params are synced to StackState's `remote_binding_params`.
Default: false (prevents sensitive data in synced state).

resources.description: Map of resource IDs to their configurations and lifecycle settings

supportedPlatforms.description: Which platforms this stack supports. When None, all platforms are supported.

registryAccessGranted.description:
Whether cross-account registry access has been successfully granted.
Set to true after the manager successfully sets the ECR/GAR repo policy
for this deployment's target account. Prevents redundant API calls on
every reconcile tick.

stackSettings.description:
User-customizable deployment settings specified at deploy time.

These settings are provided by the customer via CloudFormation parameters,
Terraform attributes, CLI flags, or Helm values. They customize how the
deployment runs and what capabilities are enabled.

**Key distinction**: StackSettings is user-customizable, while ManagementConfig
is platform-derived (from the Manager's ServiceAccount).

config.description: Resource that can hold any resource type in the Alien system. All resources share common 'type' and 'id' fields with additional type-specific properties.

id.description: The unique identifier for this specific resource instance. Must contain only alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores ([A-Za-z0-9-_]). Maximum 64 characters.

type.description: Resource type identifier that determines the specific kind of resource. This field is used for polymorphic deserialization and resource-specific behavior.

dependencies.description:
Additional dependencies for this resource beyond those defined in the resource itself.
The total dependencies are: resource.get_dependencies() + this list

items.description:
New ResourceRef that works with any resource type.
This can eventually replace the enum-based ResourceRef for full extensibility.

type.description: Resource type identifier that determines the specific kind of resource. This field is used for polymorphic deserialization and resource-specific behavior.

lifecycle.description: Describes the lifecycle of a resource within a stack, determining how it's managed and deployed.

remoteAccess.description:
Enable remote bindings for this resource (BYOB use case).
When true, binding params are synced to StackState's `remote_binding_params`.
Default: false (prevents sensitive data in synced state).

resources.description: Map of resource IDs to their configurations and lifecycle settings

supportedPlatforms.description: Which platforms this stack supports. When None, all platforms are supported.

lowercase for consistency with `CommandTargetType`.

managerUrl.description:
Manager base URL (e.g., "https://manager.alien.dev").

The manager IS the container registry — its `/v2/` endpoint serves as
the OCI Distribution API. Controllers derive the proxy host from this
to configure pull auth (RegistryCredentials, imagePullSecrets).

When None (e.g., `alien dev`), controllers use image URIs as-is.

oneOf.description:
OTLP log export configuration for a deployment.

When set, injected compute runtimes export captured application logs
through the given endpoint via OTLP/HTTP; which resources are injected
is platform-dependent. Workers and daemons read auth headers from a
runtime-only secret — never from application environment variables.
Containers have no runtime wrapper, so they get the endpoint and auth
header as plain OTEL env vars for the application's own exporter.

logsAuthHeader.description:
Auth header value in "key=value,..." format.
Example: "authorization=Bearer <write-token>"

logsEndpoint.description:
Full OTLP logs endpoint URL.
Example: "https://<manager-host>/v1/logs"

metricsAuthHeader.description:
Auth header value for the metrics endpoint in "key=value,..." format (optional).

When absent, `logs_auth_header` is reused for metrics -- suitable when the same
credential covers both signals. When present (e.g. Axiom with separate datasets),
this value is used exclusively for metrics.

Example: "authorization=Bearer <token>,x-axiom-dataset=<metrics-dataset>"

metricsEndpoint.description:
Full OTLP metrics endpoint URL (optional).
When set, the worker runtime exports its own VM/container orchestration metrics here.
Example: "https://api.axiom.co/v1/metrics"

resourceAttributes.description:
Resource attributes attached to every OTLP signal emitted for this deployment.

Platform managers use this for stable identity such as `alien.workspace_id`,
`alien.project_id`, `alien.deployment_group_id`, and `alien.deployment_id`.
Runtime-specific resource attributes such as `service.name` remain owned by
the runtime/exporter.

nativeImageHost.description:
Native image registry host+prefix for platforms that require it.

Lambda (ECR) and Cloud Run (GAR) require native registry URIs. Other
runtimes, including Azure Container Apps, pull through the manager's
registry proxy.

Derived by the manager from the artifact registry binding:
- ECR: `{account_id}.dkr.ecr.{region}.amazonaws.com/{repository_prefix}`
- GAR: `{region}-docker.pkg.dev/{project_id}/{repository_name}`

observeAllNamespaces.description:
When true the observe pass reports raw resources across every namespace
(cluster scope); otherwise it stays within the operator's own namespace.
The label selector, if any, still filters within whichever scope applies.
Ignored by cloud observers.

observeLabelSelector.description:
Kubernetes label selector that narrows which raw resources the observe
pass reports (e.g. `app.kubernetes.io/part-of=my-app`). `None` observes
everything in the namespace. Ignored by cloud observers.

publicEndpoints.description:
Public endpoint URLs for exposed resources (optional override).

Use this only when a caller already knows the public URL. Managed public
endpoint flows should prefer `domain_metadata` plus controller-reported
load balancer outputs so DNS, certificate renewal, and route readiness
stay tied to the resource state.

If not set, platforms determine public endpoint URLs from other sources:
- Managed DNS/TLS flows: `domain_metadata` FQDN or load balancer DNS
- Local: `http://localhost:{allocated_port}`
- Custom or disabled exposure: no public endpoint URL unless a controller reports one

Outer key: resource ID. Inner key: endpoint name. Value: public URL.

stackSettings.description:
User-customizable deployment settings specified at deploy time.

These settings are provided by the customer via CloudFormation parameters,
Terraform attributes, CLI flags, or Helm values. They customize how the
deployment runs and what capabilities are enabled.

**Key distinction**: StackSettings is user-customizable, while ManagementConfig
is platform-derived (from the Manager's ServiceAccount).

oneOf.description:
Deployment-time compute choices for Alien-managed compute pools.

Application source declares portable pool requirements. This settings
object stores the concrete choices made for one deployment, such as the
provider machine type and selected machine counts.

additionalProperties.description: User-selected deployment settings for one compute pool.

machine.description: Provider machine type selected for this deployment.
Provider machine type selected for this deployment.

pools.description: Selected compute choices keyed by pool ID.

deploymentModel.description: Deployment model: how updates are delivered to the remote environment.

oneOf.description:
Domain configuration for the stack.

When `custom_domains` is set, the specified resources use customer-provided
domains and certificates. Otherwise, Alien auto-generates domains.

additionalProperties.description: Custom domain configuration for a single resource.

certificate.description: Platform-specific certificate references for custom domains.

namespace.description: Secret namespace. Defaults to the release namespace when omitted.

domain.description: Fully qualified domain name to use.

customDomains.description: Custom domain configuration per resource ID.

cnameTarget.description: DNS name or URL for the external load balancer.

externalBindings.description:
External bindings for pre-existing infrastructure.
Allows using existing resources (MinIO, Redis, shared Container Apps
Environment, etc.) instead of having Alien provision them.
Required for Kubernetes platform, optional for cloud platforms.

heartbeats.description: How heartbeat health checks are handled.

oneOf.description:
Kubernetes runtime substrate configuration.

This controls how setup chooses the cluster backing `Platform::Kubernetes`
deployments. When omitted, cloud-backed Kubernetes deployments default to a
managed cluster and generic/on-prem Kubernetes defaults to an external
cluster.
Kubernetes cluster setup settings.
Optional provider-specific identity for a cloud-backed Kubernetes cluster.

cidr.description:
VPC/VNet CIDR block. If not specified, auto-generated from stack ID
to reduce conflicts (e.g., "10.{hash}.0.0/16").

public_subnet_ids.description: IDs of public subnets (required for public ingress)

security_group_ids.description: Optional security group IDs to use

vpc_id.description: The ID of the existing VPC

network_name.description: The name of the existing VPC network

region.description: The region of the subnet

subnet_name.description: The name of the subnet to use

application_gateway_subnet_name.description: Name of the dedicated classic Application Gateway subnet within the VNet.

private_endpoint_subnet_name.description:
Name of the dedicated subnet that hosts Private Endpoints (e.g. for a
Postgres Flexible Server). A Private Endpoint must not share the private
subnet, which is already claimed by the Container Apps environment's
`infrastructure_subnet_id`. Required only when the stack contains a
Postgres resource; otherwise unused.

private_subnet_name.description: Name of the private subnet within the VNet

public_subnet_name.description: Name of the public subnet within the VNet

vnet_resource_id.description: The full resource ID of the existing VNet

oneOf.description:
Canonical error container that provides a structured way to represent errors
with rich metadata including error codes, human-readable messages, context,
and chaining capabilities for error propagation.

This struct is designed to be both machine-readable and user-friendly,
supporting serialization for API responses and detailed error reporting
in distributed systems.

code.description:
A unique identifier for the type of error.

This should be a short, machine-readable string that can be used
by clients to programmatically handle different error types.
Examples: "NOT_FOUND", "VALIDATION_ERROR", "TIMEOUT"

context.description:
Additional diagnostic information about the error context.

This optional field can contain structured data providing more details
about the error, such as validation errors, request parameters that
caused the issue, or other relevant context information.

httpStatusCode.description:
HTTP status code for this error.

Used when converting the error to an HTTP response. If None, falls back to
the error type's default status code or 500.

internal.description:
Indicates if this is an internal error that should not be exposed to users.

When `true`, this error contains sensitive information or implementation
details that should not be shown to end-users. Such errors should be
logged for debugging but replaced with generic error messages in responses.

message.description:
Human-readable error message.

This message should be clear and actionable for developers or end-users,
providing context about what went wrong and potentially how to fix it.

retryable.description:
Indicates whether the operation that caused the error should be retried.

When `true`, the error is transient and the operation might succeed
if attempted again. When `false`, retrying the same operation is
unlikely to succeed without changes.

source.description:
The underlying error that caused this error, creating an error chain.

This allows for proper error propagation and debugging by maintaining
the full context of how an error occurred through multiple layers
of an application.

protocolVersion.description:
Protocol version for cross-actor compatibility.
All actors (manager, push client, agent) check this before stepping.
Mismatched versions produce a clear error instead of silent corruption.
See docs/02-manager/10-deployment-protocol.md.

retryRequested.description:
Whether a retry has been requested for a failed deployment
When true and status is a failed state, the deployment system will retry failed resources

oneOf.description:
Runtime metadata for deployment

Stores deployment state that needs to persist across step calls.

lastSyncedEnvVarsHash.description:
Hash of the environment variables snapshot that was last synced to the vault
Used to avoid redundant sync operations during incremental deployment

error.description: Latest error information if the deployment is in a failed state

code.description:
A unique identifier for the type of error.

This should be a short, machine-readable string that can be used
by clients to programmatically handle different error types.
Examples: "NOT_FOUND", "VALIDATION_ERROR", "TIMEOUT"

context.description:
Additional diagnostic information about the error context.

This optional field can contain structured data providing more details
about the error, such as validation errors, request parameters that
caused the issue, or other relevant context information.

httpStatusCode.description:
HTTP status code for this error.

Used when converting the error to an HTTP response. If None, falls back to
the error type's default status code or 500.

internal.description:
Indicates if this is an internal error that should not be exposed to users.

When `true`, this error contains sensitive information or implementation
details that should not be shown to end-users. Such errors should be
logged for debugging but replaced with generic error messages in responses.

message.description:
Human-readable error message.

This message should be clear and actionable for developers or end-users,
providing context about what went wrong and potentially how to fix it.

retryable.description:
Indicates whether the operation that caused the error should be retried.

When `true`, the error is transient and the operation might succeed
if attempted again. When `false`, retrying the same operation is
unlikely to succeed without changes.

source.description:
The underlying error that caused this error, creating an error chain.

This allows for proper error propagation and debugging by maintaining
the full context of how an error occurred through multiple layers
of an application.

oneOf.description:
Canonical error container that provides a structured way to represent errors
with rich metadata including error codes, human-readable messages, context,
and chaining capabilities for error propagation.

This struct is designed to be both machine-readable and user-friendly,
supporting serialization for API responses and detailed error reporting
in distributed systems.

code.description:
A unique identifier for the type of error.

This should be a short, machine-readable string that can be used
by clients to programmatically handle different error types.
Examples: "NOT_FOUND", "VALIDATION_ERROR", "TIMEOUT"

context.description:
Additional diagnostic information about the error context.

This optional field can contain structured data providing more details
about the error, such as validation errors, request parameters that
caused the issue, or other relevant context information.

httpStatusCode.description:
HTTP status code for this error.

Used when converting the error to an HTTP response. If None, falls back to
the error type's default status code or 500.

internal.description:
Indicates if this is an internal error that should not be exposed to users.

When `true`, this error contains sensitive information or implementation
details that should not be shown to end-users. Such errors should be
logged for debugging but replaced with generic error messages in responses.

message.description:
Human-readable error message.

This message should be clear and actionable for developers or end-users,
providing context about what went wrong and potentially how to fix it.

retryable.description:
Indicates whether the operation that caused the error should be retried.

When `true`, the error is transient and the operation might succeed
if attempted again. When `false`, retrying the same operation is
unlikely to succeed without changes.

source.description:
The underlying error that caused this error, creating an error chain.

This allows for proper error propagation and debugging by maintaining
the full context of how an error occurred through multiple layers
of an application.

lastFailedState.description:
Stores the controller state that failed, used for manual retry operations.
This allows resuming from the exact point where the failure occurred.
Stored as JSON to make the struct serializable and movable to alien-core.

id.description: The unique identifier for this specific resource instance. Must contain only alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores ([A-Za-z0-9-_]). Maximum 64 characters.

type.description: Resource type identifier that determines the specific kind of resource. This field is used for polymorphic deserialization and resource-specific behavior.

remoteBindingParams.description:
Binding parameters for remote access.
Only populated when the resource has `remote_access: true` in its ResourceEntry.
This is the JSON serialization of the binding configuration (e.g., StorageBinding, VaultBinding).
Populated by controllers during provisioning using get_binding_params().

retryAttempt.description: Tracks consecutive retry attempts for the current state transition.

status.description: Represents the high-level status of a resource during its lifecycle.

type.description: The high-level type of the resource (e.g., Worker::RESOURCE_TYPE, Storage::RESOURCE_TYPE).

resources.description: The state of individual resources, keyed by resource ID.

status.description:
Deployment status in the deployment lifecycle.

For observe-only deployments with no release or stack state, `Running`
means the Operator is attached. Connectivity comes from `lastHeartbeatAt`;
resource health comes from inventory and resource heartbeat data.

deploymentId.description: Deployment ID to reconcile state for

observedInventoryBatches.description: Observed raw-resource inventory batches read during this step.

backend.description: Backend whose observer produced this snapshot.

complete.description:
Whether this batch is a complete replacement for the scope. Complete
batches tombstone previously observed rows in the same scope when they
are absent from `resources`.

inventoryScope.description: Stable scope for the provider list operation that produced this batch.

observedAt.description: Time the inventory scope was observed.

providerKind.description:
Provider-native kind, such as `apps/v1/Deployment`,
`AWS::S3::Bucket`, `storage.googleapis.com/Bucket`, or an Azure
resource type.

rawIdentity.description:
Provider-native stable identity: Kubernetes object identity, cloud ARN,
GCP full resource name, Azure resource id, etc.

oneOf.description: Resource type identifier that determines the specific kind of resource. This field is used for polymorphic deserialization and resource-specific behavior.

version.description: Release/version identity observed from the provider resource, when available.

sourceKind.description:
Writer/source for this inventory pass, such as `operator` or
`manager-observer`.

operatorVersion.description: Operator binary version reported by the runtime.

resourceHeartbeats.description: Latest typed resource heartbeats collected during this step.

neverPauses.description:
True when a `minCapacity: 0` instance has not reached 0 ACU over the observation
window — it is silently paying always-on prices (auto-pause verification).

oneOf.description: AWS Aurora Serverless v2 backend.
GCP Cloud SQL backend.
Azure Flexible Server backend.
Local embedded Postgres backend.

resourceId.description:
Alien resource id, such as the `alien.Container` or `alien.Storage`
resource id from the stack.

resourceType.description: Resource type identifier that determines the specific kind of resource. This field is used for polymorphic deserialization and resource-specific behavior.

session.description: Lock session (push model only) - verifies lock ownership

state.description: Complete deployment state after step() execution

oneOf.description:
Canonical error container that provides a structured way to represent errors
with rich metadata including error codes, human-readable messages, context,
and chaining capabilities for error propagation.

This struct is designed to be both machine-readable and user-friendly,
supporting serialization for API responses and detailed error reporting
in distributed systems.

code.description:
A unique identifier for the type of error.

This should be a short, machine-readable string that can be used
by clients to programmatically handle different error types.
Examples: "NOT_FOUND", "VALIDATION_ERROR", "TIMEOUT"

context.description:
Additional diagnostic information about the error context.

This optional field can contain structured data providing more details
about the error, such as validation errors, request parameters that
caused the issue, or other relevant context information.

httpStatusCode.description:
HTTP status code for this error.

Used when converting the error to an HTTP response. If None, falls back to
the error type's default status code or 500.

internal.description:
Indicates if this is an internal error that should not be exposed to users.

When `true`, this error contains sensitive information or implementation
details that should not be shown to end-users. Such errors should be
logged for debugging but replaced with generic error messages in responses.

message.description:
Human-readable error message.

This message should be clear and actionable for developers or end-users,
providing context about what went wrong and potentially how to fix it.

retryable.description:
Indicates whether the operation that caused the error should be retried.

When `true`, the error is transient and the operation might succeed
if attempted again. When `false`, retrying the same operation is
unlikely to succeed without changes.

source.description:
The underlying error that caused this error, creating an error chain.

This allows for proper error propagation and debugging by maintaining
the full context of how an error occurred through multiple layers
of an application.

protocolVersion.description:
Protocol version for cross-actor compatibility.
All actors (manager, push client, agent) check this before stepping.
Mismatched versions produce a clear error instead of silent corruption.
See docs/02-manager/10-deployment-protocol.md.

retryRequested.description:
Whether a retry has been requested for a failed deployment
When true and status is a failed state, the deployment system will retry failed resources

oneOf.description:
Runtime metadata for deployment

Stores deployment state that needs to persist across step calls.

lastSyncedEnvVarsHash.description:
Hash of the environment variables snapshot that was last synced to the vault
Used to avoid redundant sync operations during incremental deployment

oneOf.description:
Canonical error container that provides a structured way to represent errors
with rich metadata including error codes, human-readable messages, context,
and chaining capabilities for error propagation.

This struct is designed to be both machine-readable and user-friendly,
supporting serialization for API responses and detailed error reporting
in distributed systems.

code.description:
A unique identifier for the type of error.

This should be a short, machine-readable string that can be used
by clients to programmatically handle different error types.
Examples: "NOT_FOUND", "VALIDATION_ERROR", "TIMEOUT"

context.description:
Additional diagnostic information about the error context.

This optional field can contain structured data providing more details
about the error, such as validation errors, request parameters that
caused the issue, or other relevant context information.

httpStatusCode.description:
HTTP status code for this error.

Used when converting the error to an HTTP response. If None, falls back to
the error type's default status code or 500.

internal.description:
Indicates if this is an internal error that should not be exposed to users.

When `true`, this error contains sensitive information or implementation
details that should not be shown to end-users. Such errors should be
logged for debugging but replaced with generic error messages in responses.

message.description:
Human-readable error message.

This message should be clear and actionable for developers or end-users,
providing context about what went wrong and potentially how to fix it.

retryable.description:
Indicates whether the operation that caused the error should be retried.

When `true`, the error is transient and the operation might succeed
if attempted again. When `false`, retrying the same operation is
unlikely to succeed without changes.

source.description:
The underlying error that caused this error, creating an error chain.

This allows for proper error propagation and debugging by maintaining
the full context of how an error occurred through multiple layers
of an application.

protocolVersion.description:
Protocol version for cross-actor compatibility.
All actors (manager, push client, agent) check this before stepping.
Mismatched versions produce a clear error instead of silent corruption.
See docs/02-manager/10-deployment-protocol.md.

retryRequested.description:
Whether a retry has been requested for a failed deployment
When true and status is a failed state, the deployment system will retry failed resources

oneOf.description:
Runtime metadata for deployment

Stores deployment state that needs to persist across step calls.

lastSyncedEnvVarsHash.description:
Hash of the environment variables snapshot that was last synced to the vault
Used to avoid redundant sync operations during incremental deployment

status.description:
Deployment status in the deployment lifecycle.

For observe-only deployments with no release or stack state, `Running`
means the Operator is attached. Connectivity comes from `lastHeartbeatAt`;
resource health comes from inventory and resource heartbeat data.

config.description: Resource that can hold any resource type in the Alien system. All resources share common 'type' and 'id' fields with additional type-specific properties.

id.description: The unique identifier for this specific resource instance. Must contain only alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores ([A-Za-z0-9-_]). Maximum 64 characters.

type.description: Resource type identifier that determines the specific kind of resource. This field is used for polymorphic deserialization and resource-specific behavior.

dependencies.description:
Additional dependencies for this resource beyond those defined in the resource itself.
The total dependencies are: resource.get_dependencies() + this list

items.description:
New ResourceRef that works with any resource type.
This can eventually replace the enum-based ResourceRef for full extensibility.

type.description: Resource type identifier that determines the specific kind of resource. This field is used for polymorphic deserialization and resource-specific behavior.

lifecycle.description: Describes the lifecycle of a resource within a stack, determining how it's managed and deployed.

remoteAccess.description:
Enable remote bindings for this resource (BYOB use case).
When true, binding params are synced to StackState's `remote_binding_params`.
Default: false (prevents sensitive data in synced state).

resources.description: Map of resource IDs to their configurations and lifecycle settings

supportedPlatforms.description: Which platforms this stack supports. When None, all platforms are supported.

target.description: Target deployment if update is needed

config.description:
Deployment configuration

Configuration for how to perform the deployment.
Note: Credentials (ClientConfig) are passed separately to step() function.

allowFrozenChanges.description:
Allow frozen resource changes during updates
When true, skips the frozen resources compatibility check.
This requires running with elevated cloud credentials.

externalBindings.description:
Map from resource ID to external binding.

Validated at runtime: binding type must match resource type.

labelDomain.description:
DNS-style label domain used for Kubernetes resource ownership labels.

Defaults to `alien.dev` when absent. Whitelabeled Operator builds set this
so generated workloads and optional log collectors share the same label
namespace.

allOf.description: AWS management configuration extracted from stack settings

managingRoleArn.description: The managing AWS IAM role ARN that can assume cross-account roles

allOf.description: GCP management configuration extracted from stack settings

serviceAccountEmail.description: Service account email for management roles

allOf.description: Azure management configuration extracted from stack settings

managingTenantId.description: The managing Azure Tenant ID for cross-tenant access

oidcIssuer.description: OIDC issuer URL trusted by the target-side managed identity.

oidcSubject.description: OIDC subject claim trusted by the target-side managed identity.

managerUrl.description:
Manager base URL (e.g., "https://manager.alien.dev").

The manager IS the container registry — its `/v2/` endpoint serves as
the OCI Distribution API. Controllers derive the proxy host from this
to configure pull auth (RegistryCredentials, imagePullSecrets).

When None (e.g., `alien dev`), controllers use image URIs as-is.

oneOf.description:
OTLP log export configuration for a deployment.

When set, injected compute runtimes export captured application logs
through the given endpoint via OTLP/HTTP; which resources are injected
is platform-dependent. Workers and daemons read auth headers from a
runtime-only secret — never from application environment variables.
Containers have no runtime wrapper, so they get the endpoint and auth
header as plain OTEL env vars for the application's own exporter.

logsAuthHeader.description:
Auth header value in "key=value,..." format.
Example: "authorization=Bearer <write-token>"

logsEndpoint.description:
Full OTLP logs endpoint URL.
Example: "https://<manager-host>/v1/logs"

metricsAuthHeader.description:
Auth header value for the metrics endpoint in "key=value,..." format (optional).

When absent, `logs_auth_header` is reused for metrics -- suitable when the same
credential covers both signals. When present (e.g. Axiom with separate datasets),
this value is used exclusively for metrics.

Example: "authorization=Bearer <token>,x-axiom-dataset=<metrics-dataset>"

metricsEndpoint.description:
Full OTLP metrics endpoint URL (optional).
When set, the worker runtime exports its own VM/container orchestration metrics here.
Example: "https://api.axiom.co/v1/metrics"

resourceAttributes.description:
Resource attributes attached to every OTLP signal emitted for this deployment.

Platform managers use this for stable identity such as `alien.workspace_id`,
`alien.project_id`, `alien.deployment_group_id`, and `alien.deployment_id`.
Runtime-specific resource attributes such as `service.name` remain owned by
the runtime/exporter.

nativeImageHost.description:
Native image registry host+prefix for platforms that require it.

Lambda (ECR) and Cloud Run (GAR) require native registry URIs. Other
runtimes, including Azure Container Apps, pull through the manager's
registry proxy.

Derived by the manager from the artifact registry binding:
- ECR: `{account_id}.dkr.ecr.{region}.amazonaws.com/{repository_prefix}`
- GAR: `{region}-docker.pkg.dev/{project_id}/{repository_name}`

observeAllNamespaces.description:
When true the observe pass reports raw resources across every namespace
(cluster scope); otherwise it stays within the operator's own namespace.
The label selector, if any, still filters within whichever scope applies.
Ignored by cloud observers.

observeLabelSelector.description:
Kubernetes label selector that narrows which raw resources the observe
pass reports (e.g. `app.kubernetes.io/part-of=my-app`). `None` observes
everything in the namespace. Ignored by cloud observers.

publicEndpoints.description:
Public endpoint URLs for exposed resources (optional override).

Use this only when a caller already knows the public URL. Managed public
endpoint flows should prefer `domain_metadata` plus controller-reported
load balancer outputs so DNS, certificate renewal, and route readiness
stay tied to the resource state.

If not set, platforms determine public endpoint URLs from other sources:
- Managed DNS/TLS flows: `domain_metadata` FQDN or load balancer DNS
- Local: `http://localhost:{allocated_port}`
- Custom or disabled exposure: no public endpoint URL unless a controller reports one

Outer key: resource ID. Inner key: endpoint name. Value: public URL.

stackSettings.description:
User-customizable deployment settings specified at deploy time.

These settings are provided by the customer via CloudFormation parameters,
Terraform attributes, CLI flags, or Helm values. They customize how the
deployment runs and what capabilities are enabled.

**Key distinction**: StackSettings is user-customizable, while ManagementConfig
is platform-derived (from the Manager's ServiceAccount).

oneOf.description:
Deployment-time compute choices for Alien-managed compute pools.

Application source declares portable pool requirements. This settings
object stores the concrete choices made for one deployment, such as the
provider machine type and selected machine counts.

additionalProperties.description: User-selected deployment settings for one compute pool.

machine.description: Provider machine type selected for this deployment.
Provider machine type selected for this deployment.

pools.description: Selected compute choices keyed by pool ID.

deploymentModel.description: Deployment model: how updates are delivered to the remote environment.

oneOf.description:
Domain configuration for the stack.

When `custom_domains` is set, the specified resources use customer-provided
domains and certificates. Otherwise, Alien auto-generates domains.

additionalProperties.description: Custom domain configuration for a single resource.

certificate.description: Platform-specific certificate references for custom domains.

domain.description: Fully qualified domain name to use.

cnameTarget.description: DNS name or URL for the external load balancer.

External bindings for pre-existing infrastructure.
Allows using existing resources (MinIO, Redis, shared Container Apps
Environment, etc.) instead of having Alien provision them.
Required for Kubernetes platform, optional for cloud platforms.

heartbeats.description: How heartbeat health checks are handled.

Kubernetes runtime substrate configuration.

This controls how setup chooses the cluster backing `Platform::Kubernetes`
deployments. When omitted, cloud-backed Kubernetes deployments default to a
managed cluster and generic/on-prem Kubernetes defaults to an external
cluster.
Kubernetes cluster setup settings.
Optional provider-specific identity for a cloud-backed Kubernetes cluster.

Permission profile that maps resources to permission sets

profiles.description:
Permission profiles that define access control for compute services
Key is the profile name, value is the permission configuration

config.description: Resource that can hold any resource type in the Alien system. All resources share common 'type' and 'id' fields with additional type-specific properties.

id.description: The unique identifier for this specific resource instance. Must contain only alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores ([A-Za-z0-9-_]). Maximum 64 characters.

type.description: Resource type identifier that determines the specific kind of resource. This field is used for polymorphic deserialization and resource-specific behavior.

dependencies.description:
Additional dependencies for this resource beyond those defined in the resource itself.
The total dependencies are: resource.get_dependencies() + this list

items.description:
New ResourceRef that works with any resource type.
This can eventually replace the enum-based ResourceRef for full extensibility.

type.description: Resource type identifier that determines the specific kind of resource. This field is used for polymorphic deserialization and resource-specific behavior.

lifecycle.description: Describes the lifecycle of a resource within a stack, determining how it's managed and deployed.

remoteAccess.description:
Enable remote bindings for this resource (BYOB use case).
When true, binding params are synced to StackState's `remote_binding_params`.
Default: false (prevents sensitive data in synced state).

resources.description: Map of resource IDs to their configurations and lifecycle settings

supportedPlatforms.description: Which platforms this stack supports. When None, all platforms are supported.

UpdateAPIKeyRequest.description: Request schema for updating an API key

expiresAt.description: Optional expiration date for the API key

UpdateDeploymentEnvironmentVariablesRequ.description: Request schema for updating deployment environment variables

targetResources.description: Target resource patterns (null = all resources)

type.description: Variable type

maxDeployments.description: Maximum number of deployments in this deployment group

UpdateDeploymentSetupPolicy.description: Editable part of a deployment link's setup config. Locked env vars and input values are preserved.

UpdateManagerRequest.description: Request schema for updating a manager to a new release

releaseId.description: Optional release ID to update to. If not provided, the active release will be chosen

defaultManagers.description: Project default private managers for new push deployments.

title.description: Optional portal title. Defaults to the project name.

domainId.description: Selected domain for this project (null = default system domain)

repo.description: The name of the git repository

type.description: The Git Provider of the repository

rootDirectory.description: The name of a directory or relative path to the source code of your project. When null is used it will default to the project root
